claim.label: Replacement Pressure
claim.query: volunteer gaelic patriotic movement duty nation labour protestant unionist minority
claim.counter_query: protestant presbyterian minority labour civic municipal foreign travel commerce
claim.thesis: Track whether plurality and civic language are displaced by a more disciplined nationalist editorial vocabulary.

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XI NO. 8, OCTOBER 14 1905
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
‘see the coremony, and I heard that they were almost

beside the bridal party, paying the greatest attention,

of course, to the London and Paris fashions, as well as,

and there’s the rub, to the observance of the Protestant

Ritual, utterly regardless of the law of the Church for-

bidding them to be there.

Cahir, I said, is a “Bung’’ stronghold. ~ For the

2,058 inhabitants of this town of “ Gallant ’’ Tipperary,

there arve no less than about thirty-three or thirty-five

“bonded stores’’ that cost the town’s people and their

neighbours over £12,000 yearly, a fact that made me

often wonder does every man, woman and child in

Cahir drink “bung.”” I should not be surprised if

I were told they do. Sunday is a thirsty day in many

places in Ireland. It is especially so in Cahir. I

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XI NO. 10, OCTOBER 28 1905
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
town is much the same. To, however, help the casual

reader to form an idea of the sort of population it now

contains I will give a few facts and figures relative to

same.

As I said (Chap. I.) Chichester’s earlier importations

were Englishmen, and the town remained principally

an English town for some time. Its trade, however,

being mostly with Scotland, and the Planters pouring

into the neighbouring counties being Scotch, Belfast be-

came by degrees Scotch too. The Irish naturally did

not get much of a look in during these early years. It

was not the place for them, judging by the poetic

address of the townspeople to William of Orange on

his arrival to fight James: —

““ Ah! mighty Prince, how do our sorrows swell,

What tongues or pens can our great miseries tell,

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. IX NO. 25, FEBRUARY 11 1905
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
tributed, why does it not issue its Statement of Ac-

counts to the public? These notes in this paper

that was puffed by the General Secretary of the

Gaelic League, say:—“ The great- industrial re-

vival that has spread over the country is a direct

outcome of the Gaelic League propaganda, and

Irish merchants and manufacturers in contributing

to the upkeep of the League will be only pay-

ing back a little of a great deal in the shape of in-

creased business prosperity that they owe to the Lan-

guage movement.” Of course, as owr readers know, the

“ great industrial revival ” is mainly the direct outcome

of the sustained campaign of the Leaper. For our

part we think it of little interest now to whose efforts

such meagre Industrial Revival as this country has

contradictory_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XI NO. 3, SEPTEMBER 9 1905
contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
for sale in some of the largest wholesale grocers’ win-

dows. Glasgow sweets and foreign chocolate were also

in evidence. He noticed in several drapers’ windows

an exhortation to wear Irvish hats, and on calling into

some of the places the first hat he was havded was

stamped “ Superior, London manufacture.’”’

Tho Bishop of Raphoe is to preach a charity sermon

at Glenties on the first Sunday in October. The object

of the appeal is to help to clear off a debt of some

£2,600 that remains on the new Parochial House,

Glenties, and on the church in process of erection for

the accommodation of the people of Croaghs.  The

Parish Priest of Glenties is Canon M‘Fadden, and

Canon M‘Fadden, of Glentics, is the Father M‘Fadden

whose name is linked in rather recent history with the

contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
to H 83, this office. Wanted, for Down, Yard and

General Farm Man, married, young, Protestant; must

be able to milk. Address ‘Z 1480, Man,’’ this office.

Wanted at once for Tipperary, a General Indoor Man,

I1.C.; must understand Butler’s work. Address ‘Z

1601, Man,’ this office. Wanted, for a Fire and Life

Insurance Offico, a Junior Clerk, Protestant, who knows

shorthand and can typewrite; age not exceeding 16.

Apply in own handwriting to H 105, this office.”

[page 2]
We now point out to Alf that these stories are not

‘“lacking in precision.”” Our libelling contemporary

irritated no doubt by what it called the “crass stu-

pidity of this campaign against the outward symbols of

loyalty to the King’’ at the recent Conferring Day,

contextual_chunks.title: The Leader, vol. XI no. 19, December 30 1905
contextual_chunks.text:
o ‘DﬁCEMEtp 30, 1905.

Jjot more than their rights, there would bo less of this
talk—this “electioneering dodge,”” as Lord Dunraven
might call it. The “Idolators’ have been so tame and
hmorogs that the exploiters of this “ electioneering
dodge instinctively feel that it is safe to play. this
trump card about “ Romish ** persecution if Home Rule
existed. So long as these bigots and oxploiters of this

electioncering dodge ’’ try to. keep, Ireland from her
rights by shouts of this kind, it is all the more incum-
bent on the persecuted majority of this country to man:
fully defend their just civic rights, and fight for them.
The utterly sectarian Mr. Sloan can stand for “ Pro-
testantism *’ over and above all; but if a base “Idola-
tor’” asks a question concerning the secular rights of un-
fur@unnte employés of the Great “Sick’’ and Sourface
Railway, Brother Goulding, of the Freemasons, and
others cry out against “dragging religion into business.’””
The more weak people foolishly “prove their toler-
ance,”” the more they may expect to bo jumped on ; when
they stand up manfully to their just fight they will very
quickly be treated with.

The Mungret Annual for

maz season of the year s usually one of gladness and re-
Joleing, it is also only too often a period of moaning and
groaning for those afilicted with coughs, colds, pains,
aches, and the many other ills to which the flesh is heir.
As one lays in a stock of good things to comfort the
}m@y and to appease and to please the appetite, so, too,
T is wiso to lay in a preventative against ailments.”’
Alas poor Pink! Bub away with all these thoughts
about pains and aches; the next shop visited was—well,
the reader can judge for himself what it was:—The
well-known licensed establishment of , which has
long since made its name in Dublin, continues to enjoy
a bigh reputation for the excellence of its liquors, and
e purity and good condition of its stouts and beers.”’

Perhaps some of our Wexford readers can inform us
it the Slaney mills have been re-opened.  We learn
from a correspondent that a traveller called on him
sonre time ago and left a card containing the announce-
went @ “Encouvage Ivish manufacture. Ryan Doyle has
opeuad Slaney mills, Co. Wexford, for the manufacture
